etymology.description: 戈(戈) (gē, "blade") + 止(止) (zhǐ, "foot; to walk; to stop") = army going on an expedition -> later associated with just warfare
etymology.interpretation: The character 武 originally combines the idea of a blade (戈) with movement or marching (止), representing an army on the move. While traditionally reinterpreted as 'to stop violence', linguistically it aligns better with the idea of martial activity or skill. The connection to just warfare is a later reinterpretation.

examples.chinese: 他喜歡看武俠小說(他喜歡看武俠小說)。
examples.english: He likes to read martial arts novels.

examples.chinese: 我們去看了一場武術表演(我們去看了一場武術表演)。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen qù kànle yī chǎng wǔshù biǎoyǎn.
examples.english: We went to watch a martial arts performance.

examples.chinese: 他正在學習武功招式(他正在學習武功招式)。
examples.english: He is learning martial arts moves.
